title: Per capita, Alaska tops NRA rolls; more joining
summary: Per capita, Alaska tops NRA rolls; more joining By MOLLY BROWN Daily News reporter In Alaska, about 28,000 people belong to the National Rifle Association and most have life memberships, said Janey Wineinger, the Alaska NRA field representative. Per capita, Alaska and Wyoming have the most people registered in the powerful U.S. gun lobby group, she said. The organization watches state, local and federal activity for anti-gun legislation, said Wayne Ross, an Anchorage lawyer who represents Alaska on the NRA board with Rep. Don Young. Just last week, an NRA lobbyist from California testified in Juneau regarding concealed-carry licenses and held a reception for state legislators, Ross said. On Thursday, a national officer of the NRA will arrive in Alaska and speak to members in Valdez, Glenna…

title: On course for a record win
summary: On course for a record win Swift Swingley set to break Iditarod's nine-day barrier By CRAIG MEDRED Daily News outdoors editor WHITE MOUNTAIN - Expect Montanan Doug Swingley to challenge the Iditarod Trail Sled Dog Race's nine-day barrier in Nome this morning. After he pulled in here Monday afternoon with the sun still high in the western sky, he confessed, "I'm kind of at least giving it a try." If he succeeds, Swingley may be headed down Nome's Front Street before noon. Defending champion and holder of the Iditarod record of 9 days, 2 hours, Swingley came into this race confident and predicting victory. By Monday, he had just 77 miles left to make the prediction come true. After Swingley's mandatory eight-hour rest here ends at 1:33 a.m. today, he could try to extend his six-to-eight-hour…

title: Tanana Chiefs alcohol vote angers Minto leader
summary: Tanana Chiefs alcohol vote angers Minto leader By MARTHA BRISTOW Fairbanks Daily News-Miner FAIRBANKS - Within a week, the name of Minto's traditional leader, Chief Peter John, will be taken off the front of the Tanana Chiefs Conference tribal hall here. The chief asked that his name be removed after conference delegates voted to allow alcohol consumption at non-Tanana Chiefs Conference functions within the hall to increase revenue. They also voted to appropriate $1 million from the conference's fund balance to construct a new building, a "true" tribal hall for Native gatherings. Alcohol will be prohibited at the new building. The 25-to-13 vote came after two hours of closed-door debate. Those who were not delegates or tribal members stood outside in the March sunshine speculating about th…

title: Swingley's great race
summary: Swingley's great race Montana musher sets speed mark in his third win By LEW FREEDMAN Daily News sports editor NOME - There is big history and there is temporary history. Tuesday, mushing the final miles of the 28th annual Iditarod Trail Sled Dog Race, Doug Swingley tried to write history that would stand forever. Over the last 77 miles from White Mountain, Swingley wanted to squeeze one more thing from a race he dominated. He sought to become the first musher to run an eight-day Iditarod. "It would have been a milestone," said Swingley. He almost made it. Crossing under the new finish arch on Front Street just before 11 a.m., Swingley completed a record run from Anchorage to Nome in 9 days, 58 minutes and 6 seconds. He broke three records he already held, but just missed the barrier-break…
